page.title: Hepatitis C — RepurpOS | OpenSourceMedicine
page.description: Structured alterations and therapeutics for Hepatitis C: molecular targets, clinical biomarkers, phenotypes, and ranked drugs from Open Targets, HPO, ChEMBL, and DGIdb.
page.hero: Disease intelligence for Hepatitis C: 107 alterations and 357 ranked therapeutics from curated public databases.

gmi_articles.title: Adverse reactions to telaprevir are common, e.g. maculopapular rash (52% in 1 trail) and anemia (27% in 1 trial).
Cesarean delivery does not decrease perinatal hepatitis C transmission from Hepatitis C positive mothers to infants.
Effect of L-carnitine supplementation on lipid profiles of patients with liver disease.
A triple antioxidant approach to the treatment of hepatitis C may be superior to traditional interferon and antiviral drug therapy.
A lead in course with intravenous silibinin prior to the initiation of triple therapy resulted in a 63% sustained virologic response rate in 10/16 HIV/HCV coinfected patients.
A low-iron diet may have therapeutic value in chronic hepatitis C and alcoholic/nonalcoholic steatohepatitis.
A mixture of milk thistle and phosphatidylcholine (LiverPro) has therapeutic activity in patients with chronic viral hepatitis C.
A multi antioxidative treatment in chronic hepatitis C virus patients is well tolerated and therapeutic.
Acute hepatitis C in Israel is predominantly an iatrogenic disease.
Addition of epigallocatechin gallate 400 mg to sofosbuvir 400 mg + daclatisvir 60 mg in in treatment of patients with chronic hepatitis C.
Antioxidant therapy is beneficial for chronic hepatitis C patients after failure of interferon.
Arabinoxylan rice bran (Biobran) suppresses the viremia level in patients with chronic HCV infection.
Artichoke leave extract significantly improves fatigue and joint problems in patients with Hepatitis C.
Beneficial effects of silybin treatment after viral eradication in patients with HCV.
Camel milk had a regulatory effect on multiple parameters of inflammatory mediators, immunomodulators, antiapoptosis, and antioxidants.
Cannabis use is associated with a lower insulin resistance risk in HIV-HCV-coinfected patients.
Chlorella supplementation is safe and efficacious in adults with chronic hepatitis C virus infection.
Eicosapentaenoic acid supplementation may be useful in therapy for chronic hepatitis C.
Enhanced efficacy of direct-acting antivirals in hepatitis C patients by coadministration of black cumin and ascorbate.
EPA ameliorates the impaired filterability of erythrocytes from patients with chronic hepatitis C.
EPA may increase the efficacy and/or reduce ribavirin-associated hemolytic anemia in patients being treated for chronic hepatitic C virus infection.
EPA supplementation has therapeutic value in the treatment of chronic hepatitis C patients.
Expression patterns of exosomal miRNAs are a promising tool to predict sustained viral response- hepatocellular carcinoma.
Folic acid may have a therapeutic effect in treating viral hepatitis.
Hepatitis C prevalence in Southern Italy may be due to iatrogenic transmission through the Salk Polio vaccine 1956-1965.
High-dose vitamins E and C supplementation prevents ribavirin-induced hemolytic anemia in patients with chronic hepatitis C.
Iatrogenic (medicine-caused) and not intrafamilial transmission accounts for most Hepatitis C infection among Egypitians.

Kushenin, a flavonoid compound found within Sophora flavescens, inhibits the proliferation of hepatitis C virus (HCV), protects the liver and has anti-liver fibrosis effects in patients with HSV after renal transplantation.
Lactoferrin is a potential useful adjunct treatment for patients with chronic hepatitis C.
Lactoferrin is one potential candidate as an anti‐HCV reagent that may be effective for the treatment of patients with chronic hepatitis.
Long term administration of a licorice extract in the treatment of chronic hepatitis C is effective in preventing liver carcinogenesis.
Long term administration of a licorice extract in the treatment of chronic hepatitis C is effective in preventing liver cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma.
Morbidity and mortality of recurrent hepatitis C infection after orthotopic liver transplantation is a significant problem.
N. sativa administration in patients with HCV was tolerable, safe, decreased viral load, and improved oxidative stress, clinical condition and glycemic control in diabetic patients.
Omega-3 fatty acids reduce fibrogenesis and increase responsiveness to antiviral therapy in chronic hepatitis C.
Orange juice was a convenient food in the diet of patients due to the increase in antioxidant capacity and decreased inflammation and cholesterol.
Preliminary study on therapeutic effect of oxymatrine in treating patients with chronic hepatitis C.
Prevalence of Vitamin-D deficiency is related to severity of liver damage in Hepatitis-C patients.
Proton pump inhibitors are associated with accelerated development of cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma in patients with hepatitis C infection.
Regular coffee consumption is associated with lower rates of disease progression in advanced hepatitis-C related disease.

remission.spontaneous_remission_rate: Spontaneous clearance in <30% of acute cases; chronic infection rarely clears without treatment (<1%/year)
remission.best_intervention_remission_rate: >95% cured (sustained virologic response at 12 weeks) with 8-12 week courses of direct-acting antivirals (DAAs)
remission.gap_size: Near-zero biological gap; large access/utilization gap
remission.barrier_detail: Only 65% of eligible patients with private insurance received DAA treatment as of 2020; rates declining since 2019; Medicaid restrictions remain in many states; $20,000+ drug cost for generics unavailable in US
remission.last_soc_change: 2014-2016 — DAA revolution (sofosbuvir 2013, ledipasvir/sofosbuvir 2014, pan-genotypic regimens 2016-2017); prior interferon-ribavirin standard unchanged since ~2001 with <50% efficacy and severe side effects
remission.outdated_treatments: Interferon-based regimens (now essentially obsolete but occasionally prescribed in settings with limited DAA access); ribavirin-only maintenance (no antiviral effect alone)
remission.adjunctive_emerging: Harm reduction integration (needle exchange + DAA treatment as combined strategy); primary-care-based treatment (task-shifting from hepatologist to GP dramatically increases uptake in trials)
remission.frontier_signal: HCV vaccine candidates (multiple Phase 1/2, MRC/NIH); cure for HCV reactivation in immunocompromised; ultra-short (4-week) DAA regimens under investigation
remission.notes: Manually curated from landmark trials, registries, and guidelines (disease_db_100.json). Protected from automated overwrite.
